526th Infantry Division.summary: The 526th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of the German Wehrmacht, during World War II. Assembled in October 1939, it subsequently became the Division No.
526th Infantry Division.article: The 526th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of the German Wehrmacht, during World War II. Assembled in October 1939, it subsequently became the Division No. 526, also referred to in some Wehrmacht sources as the "526th Reserve Division". It existed until early 1945.

52nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 52nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II, which would become the 52nd Field Training Division in December 1943 and then the 52nd Security Division in April 1944.
52nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 52nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II, which would become the 52nd Field Training Division in December 1943 and then the 52nd Security Division in April 1944.

538th Frontier Guard Division (Wehrmacht.article: The 538th Frontier Guard Division, also known as the Division z.b.V. 538. was a short-lived German division in World War II that participated in the invasion of Yugoslavia. It was disbanded immediately after the Yugoslav surrender.

541st Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 541st Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945.
541st Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 541st Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945.

542nd Grenadier Division.article: The 542nd Grenadier Division was a German military unit during World War II.

544th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 544th Volksgrenadier Division was a Volksgrenadier-type infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was active from July 1944 to May 1945.
544th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 544th Volksgrenadier Division was a Volksgrenadier-type infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was active from July 1944 to May 1945. In the first two months of service, the division was designated 544th Grenadier Division.

548th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 548th Grenadier Division was a German Infantry Division during World War II.
548th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 548th Grenadier Division was a German Infantry Division during World War II.

549th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 549th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945. It was formed as the 549th Grenadier Division in July 1944 and became a volksgrenadier division several months later.
549th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 549th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945. It was formed as the 549th Grenadier Division in July 1944 and became a volksgrenadier division several months later. Fighting on the Eastern Front, it was nearly destroyed in the East Prussian Offensive, with its remnants retreating west and surrendering to American troops at the end of the war.

551st Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 551st Volksgrenadier Division was a Volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945.
551st Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 551st Volksgrenadier Division was a Volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945.

553rd Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 553rd Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during World War II that fought entirely on the Western Front.
553rd Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 553rd Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during World War II that fought entirely on the Western Front.

554th Infantry Division.summary: The 554th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active for several months in the year 1940.
554th Infantry Division.article: The 554th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active for several months in the year 1940.

555th Infantry Division.summary: The 555th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was formed in February 1940 as a defensive unit in the Upper Rhine; three of its regiments were formed from existing units in other divisions and the personnel for the fourth were drawn from the Landschutz (militia).
555th Infantry Division.article: The 555th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was formed in February 1940 as a defensive unit in the Upper Rhine; three of its regiments were formed from existing units in other divisions and the personnel for the fourth were drawn from the Landschutz (militia). Because of its fragmented formation it lacked the regional identity which was found in many early war German divisions. The division played a defensive role in the Battle for France, holding positions opposite Strasbourg. It was moved into reserve in July 1940 and dissolved in September.

556th Infantry Division.summary: The 556th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active for several months in the year 1940.
556th Infantry Division.article: The 556th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active for several months in the year 1940.

557th Infantry Division.summary: The 557th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active for several months in the year 1940.
557th Infantry Division.article: The 557th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active for several months in the year 1940.

558th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 558th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945.
558th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 558th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier infantry division of the German Army during World War II, active from 1944 to 1945.

559th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 559th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during World War II that fought entirely on the Western Front.
559th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 559th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during World War II that fought entirely on the Western Front.

560th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 560th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.
560th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 560th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.

561st Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 561st Volksgrenadier Division was a division of the Wehrmacht active during World War II. When it was formed in July 1944, it was originally designated as the 561st Grenadier Division.
561st Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 561st Volksgrenadier Division was a division of the Wehrmacht active during World War II. When it was formed in July 1944, it was originally designated as the 561st Grenadier Division.

562nd Grenadier Division.summary: The 562nd Grenadier Division was an infantry division of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht.
562nd Grenadier Division.article: The 562nd Grenadier Division was an infantry division of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht.

563rd Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 563rd Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.
563rd Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 563rd Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.

564th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 564th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War that existed from 26 August to 15 September 1944.
564th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 564th Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War that existed from 26 August to 15 September 1944.

56th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 56th Infantry Division was a German infantry division which fought during World War II.
56th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 56th Infantry Division was a German infantry division which fought during World War II.

574th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 574th Volksgrenadier Division was a German military unit during World War II. It formed on 25 August 1944 from the remnants of the previously annihilated 277th Infantry Division.
574th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 574th Volksgrenadier Division was a German military unit during World War II. It formed on 25 August 1944 from the remnants of the previously annihilated 277th Infantry Division. On 4 September 1944 the unit was renamed to 277th Volksgrenadier Division.

57th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 57th Infantry Division was a German division in World War II. It was formed on 26 August 1939 in Landshut.
57th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 57th Infantry Division was a German division in World War II. It was formed on 26 August 1939 in Landshut.

58th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 58th Infantry Division was a unit of the German Army (Wehrmacht) during World War II. It was formed in 1939, took part in the Battle of France in 1940, and then Operation Barbarossa in 1941.
58th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 58th Infantry Division was a unit of the German Army (Wehrmacht) during World War II. It was formed in 1939, took part in the Battle of France in 1940, and then Operation Barbarossa in 1941. The 58th was then constantly engaged on the Eastern Front until the end of the war in 1945.

59th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 59th Infantry Division (German: 59. Infanteriedivision) was a military division of the Wehrmacht during World War II.
59th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 59th Infantry Division (German: 59. Infanteriedivision) was a military division of the Wehrmacht during World War II.

5th Flak Division.summary: The 5th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.
5th Flak Division.article: The 5th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.

5th Jäger Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The German 5th Infantry Division was formed in October 1934 and mobilized on 25 August 1939. The division's troops were garrisoned in Konstanz, Ulm, and Freiburg.
5th Jäger Division (Wehrmacht).article: The German 5th Infantry Division was formed in October 1934 and mobilized on 25 August 1939. The division's troops were garrisoned in Konstanz, Ulm, and Freiburg. When formed, the division consisted of the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd battalions of the 14th, 56th, and 75th Infantry Regiments, the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd Battalions of the 5th Artillery Regiment, the 1st battalion of the 41st Artillery Regiment, and assorted 5th Division support units.

5th Luftwaffe Field Division.summary: The 5th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. Formed using surplus Luftwaffe ground crew, it served on the Eastern Front from late 1942 to mid-1944, when it was disbanded.
5th Luftwaffe Field Division.article: The 5th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. Formed using surplus Luftwaffe ground crew, it served on the Eastern Front from late 1942 to mid-1944, when it was disbanded.

5th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 5th Mountain Division was a mountain infantry (Gebirgsjäger) formation of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was established in the Wehrkreis XVIII in October 1940, out of units taken from the 1st Mountain Division and the 10th Infantry Division.
5th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 5th Mountain Division was a mountain infantry (Gebirgsjäger) formation of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was established in the Wehrkreis XVIII in October 1940, out of units taken from the 1st Mountain Division and the 10th Infantry Division. The unit surrendered to the U.S. Army near Turin in May 1945.

5th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 5th Panzer Division was an armoured division of the German Army during World War II, established in 1938.
5th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 5th Panzer Division was an armoured division of the German Army during World War II, established in 1938.

5th Parachute Division (Germany).summary: The 5th Parachute Division was a Fallschirmjäger (paratroop) division in the German air force (Luftwaffe) during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.
5th Parachute Division (Germany).article: The 5th Parachute Division was a Fallschirmjäger (paratroop) division in the German air force (Luftwaffe) during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.

600th Infantry Division.summary: The 600th (Russian) Infantry Division, 1st lnfantry Division of the ROA (KONR) was a military division that was formed by the German Army during the World War II. It drew its men from Russian prisoners of war and forced laborers.
600th Infantry Division.article: The 600th (Russian) Infantry Division, 1st lnfantry Division of the ROA (KONR) was a military division that was formed by the German Army during the World War II. It drew its men from Russian prisoners of war and forced laborers.

606th Infantry Division.summary: The 606th Infantry Division, also known as the Division for special deployment 606 was a short-lived infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active in April 1945.
606th Infantry Division.article: The 606th Infantry Division, also known as the Division for special deployment 606 was a short-lived infantry division of the Heer, the ground forces of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht. It was active in April 1945.

60th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 60th Infantry Division was formed in late 1939, from Gruppe Eberhardt, a collection of SA units that had been engaged in the capture of Danzig during the Invasion of Poland. This division was unusual in that its manpower was largely drawn from the SA and the police.
60th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 60th Infantry Division was formed in late 1939, from Gruppe Eberhardt, a collection of SA units that had been engaged in the capture of Danzig during the Invasion of Poland. This division was unusual in that its manpower was largely drawn from the SA and the police.

61st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 61st Infantry Division was a combat division of the German Army during the Second World War. Towards the end of the war, it became the 61st Volksgrenadier Division.
61st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 61st Infantry Division was a combat division of the German Army during the Second World War. Towards the end of the war, it became the 61st Volksgrenadier Division.

62nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 62nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was formed in Wehrkreis VIII (Silesia) in August 1939.
62nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 62nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was formed in Wehrkreis VIII (Silesia) in August 1939. After heavy casualties in March 1944, it was first briefly reassembled in August 1944 and then reorganized into the 62nd Volksgrenadier Division, formed from units assembled for the planned 583rd Volksgrenadier Division, on 22 September 1944. The dissolution of 62nd Infantry Division was declared on 9 October 1944. 62nd Volksgrenadier Division remained operational until it was trapped in the Ruhr Pocket and forced to surrender by American forces in April 1945.

62nd Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 62nd Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.
62nd Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 62nd Volksgrenadier Division was a volksgrenadier division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.

63rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 63rd Infantry Division was a fictitious infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. No real division-size unit named 63rd Infantry Division was ever deployed in combat, but the name was given to a far smaller military unit.
63rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 63rd Infantry Division was a fictitious infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. No real division-size unit named 63rd Infantry Division was ever deployed in combat, but the name was given to a far smaller military unit. A reference was first made to the 63rd Infantry Division in a German Schematische Kriegsgliederung document dated 12 April 1945, where the fictitious division was listed among the reserves of Oberkommando West. In truth, the troops dubbed 63rd Infantry Division were the "Regiment Alvensleben". The code name was assigned to the regiment on 22 March 1945.

86th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The German 86th Infantry Division was created on 26 August 1939. The division was disbanded on 3 November 1943.
86th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The German 86th Infantry Division was created on 26 August 1939. The division was disbanded on 3 November 1943.

Airborne forces.summary: Airborne forces are ground combat units carried by aircraft and airdropped into battle zones, typically by parachute drop. Parachute-qualified infantry and support personnel serving in airborne units are also known as paratroopers.
Airborne forces.article: Airborne forces are ground combat units carried by aircraft and airdropped into battle zones, typically by parachute drop. Parachute-qualified infantry and support personnel serving in airborne units are also known as paratroopers.

Berlin.summary: Berlin is the capital of Germany as well as its largest city by both area and population. With 4 million inhabitants, it has the highest population within its city limits of any city in the European Union.
Berlin.article: Berlin is the capital of Germany as well as its largest city by both area and population. With 4 million inhabitants, it has the highest population within its city limits of any city in the European Union. The city is also one of the states of Germany, being the third-smallest state in the country by area. Berlin is surrounded by the state of Brandenburg, bordering Brandenburg's capital Potsdam to the southwest. The urban area of Berlin has a population of over 5 million, making it the most populous in Germany. The Berlin-Brandenburg capital region has around 6 million inhabitants and is Germany's second-largest metropolitan region after the Rhine-Ruhr region, as well as the fifth-biggest metropolitan region by GDP in the European Union.

Cavalry.summary: Historically, cavalry are groups of soldiers or warriors who fight mounted on horseback. Until the 20th century, cavalry were the most mobile of the combat arms, operating as light cavalry in the roles of reconnaissance, screening, and skirmishing, or as heavy cavalry for decisive economy of force and shock attacks.
Cavalry.article: Historically, cavalry are groups of soldiers or warriors who fight mounted on horseback. Until the 20th century, cavalry were the most mobile of the combat arms, operating as light cavalry in the roles of reconnaissance, screening, and skirmishing, or as heavy cavalry for decisive economy of force and shock attacks. An individual soldier in the cavalry is known by a number of designations depending on era and tactics, such as a cavalryman, horseman, trooper, cataphract, knight, drabant, hussar, uhlan, mamluk, cuirassier, lancer, dragoon, samurai or horse archer. The designation of cavalry was not usually given to any military forces that used other animals or platforms for mounts, such as chariots, camels or elephants. Infantry who moved on horseback, but dismounted to fight on foot, were known in the early 17th to the early 18th century as dragoons, a class of mounted infantry which in most armies later evolved into standard cavalry while retaining their historic designation.

Combined arms.summary: Combined arms is an approach to warfare that seeks to integrate different combat arms of a military to achieve mutually complementary effects—for example, using infantry and armour in an urban environment in which each supports the other.
Combined arms.article: Combined arms is an approach to warfare that seeks to integrate different combat arms of a military to achieve mutually complementary effects—for example, using infantry and armour in an urban environment in which each supports the other.

Eastern Front (World War II).summary: The Eastern Front, also known as the Great Patriotic War, or the German–Soviet War, was a theatre of World War II fought between the European Axis powers and Allies, including the Soviet Union (USSR) and Poland. It encompassed Central Europe, Eastern Europe, Northeast Europe (Baltics), and Southeast Europe (Balkans), and lasted from 22 June 1941 to 9 May 1945.
Eastern Front (World War II).article: The Eastern Front, also known as the Great Patriotic War, or the German–Soviet War, was a theatre of World War II fought between the European Axis powers and Allies, including the Soviet Union (USSR) and Poland. It encompassed Central Europe, Eastern Europe, Northeast Europe (Baltics), and Southeast Europe (Balkans), and lasted from 22 June 1941 to 9 May 1945. Of the estimated 70–85 million deaths attributed to the war, around 30 million occurred on the Eastern Front, including 9 million children. The Eastern Front was decisive in determining the outcome in the European theatre of operations in World War II and is the main cause of the defeat of Nazi Germany and the Axis nations. Historian Geoffrey Roberts noted that "more than 80 percent of all combat during the Second World War took place on the Eastern Front".

Fallschirmjäger.summary: The Fallschirmjäger were the airborne forces branch of the Luftwaffe before and during World War II. They were the first paratroopers to be committed in large-scale airborne operations.
Fallschirmjäger.article: The Fallschirmjäger were the airborne forces branch of the Luftwaffe before and during World War II. They were the first paratroopers to be committed in large-scale airborne operations. They were commanded by Kurt Student, the Luftwaffe's second-in-command.

ISBN.summary: The International Standard Book Number (ISBN) is a numeric commercial book identifier that is intended to be unique. Publishers purchase or receive ISBNs from an affiliate of the International ISBN Agency.
ISBN.article: The International Standard Book Number (ISBN) is a numeric commercial book identifier that is intended to be unique. Publishers purchase or receive ISBNs from an affiliate of the International ISBN Agency.

Kampfgruppe.summary: In military history, the German term Kampfgruppe can refer to a combat formation of any kind, but most usually to that employed by the Wehrmacht of Nazi Germany and its allies during World War II, and, to a lesser extent, the German Empire in World War I.
Kampfgruppe.article: In military history, the German term Kampfgruppe can refer to a combat formation of any kind, but most usually to that employed by the Wehrmacht of Nazi Germany and its allies during World War II, and, to a lesser extent, the German Empire in World War I.

Light infantry.summary: Light infantry refers to certain types of lightly equipped infantry throughout history. They have a more mobile or fluid function than other types of infantry, such as heavy infantry or line infantry.
Light infantry.article: Light infantry refers to certain types of lightly equipped infantry throughout history. They have a more mobile or fluid function than other types of infantry, such as heavy infantry or line infantry. Historically, light infantry often fought as scouts, raiders, and skirmishers. These are loose formations that fight ahead of the main army to harass, delay, disrupt supply lines, engage the enemy's own skirmishing forces, and generally "soften up" an enemy before the main battle. Light infantrymen were also often responsible for screening the main body of a military formation.

Military communications.summary: Military communications or military signals involve all aspects of communications, or conveyance of information, by armed forces. Examples from Jane's Military Communications include text, audio, facsimile, tactical ground-based communications, naval signalling, terrestrial microwave, tropospheric scatter, satellite communications systems and equipment, surveillance and signal analysis, security, direction finding and jamming.
Military communications.article: Military communications or military signals involve all aspects of communications, or conveyance of information, by armed forces. Examples from Jane's Military Communications include text, audio, facsimile, tactical ground-based communications, naval signalling, terrestrial microwave, tropospheric scatter, satellite communications systems and equipment, surveillance and signal analysis, security, direction finding and jamming. The most urgent purposes are to communicate information to commanders and orders from them.

Pioneer (military).summary: A pioneer is a soldier employed to perform engineering and construction tasks. The term is in principle similar to sapper or combat engineer.
Pioneer (military).article: A pioneer is a soldier employed to perform engineering and construction tasks. The term is in principle similar to sapper or combat engineer. Pioneers were originally part of the artillery branch of European armies. Subsequently, they formed part of the engineering branch, the logistic branch, part of the infantry, or even comprised a branch in their own right.

Security Division (Wehrmacht).summary: Security Divisions were German rear-area military units engaged in Nazi security warfare in occupied Europe during World War II. Almost all divisions were employed in areas on the Eastern front with the exception of the 325th Security Division which operated within occupied France.
Security Division (Wehrmacht).article: Security Divisions were German rear-area military units engaged in Nazi security warfare in occupied Europe during World War II. Almost all divisions were employed in areas on the Eastern front with the exception of the 325th Security Division which operated within occupied France. The units were tasked with fighting local partisans, intelligence, and counterinsurgency against resistance groups, rounding up Jews and other ethnic groups as part of the Holocaust, and conducting punitive actions in civilian areas.

Volkssturm.summary: The Volkssturm was a levée en masse national militia established by Nazi Germany during the last months of World War II. It was set up by the Nazi Party on the orders of Adolf Hitler and established on 25 September 1944.
Volkssturm.article: The Volkssturm was a levée en masse national militia established by Nazi Germany during the last months of World War II. It was set up by the Nazi Party on the orders of Adolf Hitler and established on 25 September 1944. It was staffed by conscripting males between the ages of 16 and 60 years who were not already serving in some military unit.

List of Waffen-SS divisions.summary: All Waffen-SS divisions were ordered in a single series of numbers as formed, regardless of type. Those with ethnic groups listed were at least nominally recruited from those groups.
List of Waffen-SS divisions.article: All Waffen-SS divisions were ordered in a single series of numbers as formed, regardless of type. Those with ethnic groups listed were at least nominally recruited from those groups. Many of the higher-numbered units were divisions in name only, being in reality only small battlegroups (Kampfgruppen).

World War II.summary: World War II, or the Second World War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated.
World War II.article: World War II, or the Second World War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers. Nearly all of the world's countries participated. Tanks and aircraft played major roles, the latter enabling the strategic bombing of cities and delivery of the only nuclear weapons used in war. World War II was the deadliest conflict in history, causing the death of 60 to 75 million people. Millions died as a result of massacres, starvation, disease, and genocides, including the Holocaust. After the Allied victory, Germany, Austria, Japan, and Korea were occupied, and German and Japanese leaders were tried for war crimes.

Raid on Darwin (2 May 1943).summary: The Japanese raid on Darwin of 2 May 1943 was a significant battle in the North Western Area Campaign of World War II. During the raid a force of over 20 Japanese bombers and Zero fighters attacked the Australian town of Darwin, Northern Territory, inflicting little damage on the ground.
Raid on Darwin (2 May 1943).article: The Japanese raid on Darwin of 2 May 1943 was a significant battle in the North Western Area Campaign of World War II. During the raid a force of over 20 Japanese bombers and Zero fighters attacked the Australian town of Darwin, Northern Territory, inflicting little damage on the ground. This attack was the 54th Japanese airstrike over Australia.

64th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 64th Infantry Division (64. Infanterie-Division) was an infantry division of the Wehrmacht during World War II.
64th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 64th Infantry Division (64. Infanterie-Division) was an infantry division of the Wehrmacht during World War II.

650th Infantry Division.summary: The 650th (Russian) Infantry Division, 2nd lnfantry Division of the ROA (KONR) was a military division that was formed by the German Army during World War II. It drew its men mainly from Russian prisoners of war.
650th Infantry Division.article: The 650th (Russian) Infantry Division, 2nd lnfantry Division of the ROA (KONR) was a military division that was formed by the German Army during World War II. It drew its men mainly from Russian prisoners of war.

65th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 65th Infantry Division was a German division in World War II. It was formed in July 1942.
65th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 65th Infantry Division was a German division in World War II. It was formed in July 1942.

68th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 68th Infantry Division was a formation of the German army during World War II. It was formed in 1939 and was initially committed to the German invasion of Poland.
68th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 68th Infantry Division was a formation of the German army during World War II. It was formed in 1939 and was initially committed to the German invasion of Poland. It took part in the Battle of France in 1940, and then Operation Barbarossa in 1941 as part of Army Group South. The 68th remained in southern Russia until refitted in Poland in early 1944. Returned to action the 68th fought for rest of the war in the East, in Russia, Slovakia, in the defence of Germany until finally surrendering to the Soviets in Czechoslovakia.

69th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 69th Infantry Division was a combat division of the German Wehrmacht during the Second World War.
69th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 69th Infantry Division was a combat division of the German Wehrmacht during the Second World War.

6th Flak Division.summary: The 6th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.
6th Flak Division.article: The 6th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.

6th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 6th Infantry Division was a unit of the German Army during World War II.
6th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 6th Infantry Division was a unit of the German Army during World War II.

6th Luftwaffe Field Division.summary: The 6th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed using surplus ground crew of the Luftwaffe at Training Ground Camp Gross-Born from Luftwaffen-Flieger Regiment 21.
6th Luftwaffe Field Division.article: The 6th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed using surplus ground crew of the Luftwaffe at Training Ground Camp Gross-Born from Luftwaffen-Flieger Regiment 21. Its initial training had been at Magdeburg. Like other Luftwaffe Field Divisions initially when first formed the unit's infantry complement contained no regimental headquarters, but did have four independently led Jager battalions. Its Artillery Battalion contained two batteries of 10.5 cm leFH 18M Towed Guns and a battery of 75mm L24 Short Barreled Sturmgeschütz III Assault Guns. The Panzer-Jager Battalion contained two batteries of 5 cm Pak 38 Anti Tank Guns and one battery of 7.5 cm Pak 40 Anti Tank Guns. The Flak Battalion contained three companies of three Batteries of four, armed with the single barreled Flak 38 2 cm Flak 30, Flak 38 and Flakvierling 38, three batteries of four of the Flak 37 3.7 cm Flak 18/36/37 and four Flak 36 guns 8.8 cm Flak 18/36/37/41. It served under Army Group Centre on the Central Sector of the Eastern Front from late 1942 to June 1944. Where it took part in the Battle of Velikiye Luki, Operation Maigewitter & the Battle of Nevel, it was destroyed during Operation Bagration.

6th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 6th Mountain Division was a German army Division of World War II. It was established in June 1940, and was deployed to France for occupation duties.
6th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 6th Mountain Division was a German army Division of World War II. It was established in June 1940, and was deployed to France for occupation duties. In December it was relocated to Poland, where it remained until the spring of 1941. Having formed XVIII Mountain Corps with the 5th Mountain Division, it then took part in Operation Marita, the invasion of Greece during the Balkans Campaign. In September it was relocated to northern Finland, where it operated in Lapland. From July 1942 onward it was part of the 20th Mountain Army along the Arctic coast. It withdrew into Norway when the Germans evacuated Finland in late 1944, and surrendered to the British at the end of the war in 1945.

6th Panzer Division.summary: The 6th Panzer Division was an armoured division in the German Army, the Heer, during World War II, established in October 1939.
6th Panzer Division.article: The 6th Panzer Division was an armoured division in the German Army, the Heer, during World War II, established in October 1939.

6th Parachute Division (Germany).summary: The 6th Parachute Division was a Fallschirmjäger (airborne) division of the German military during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.
6th Parachute Division (Germany).article: The 6th Parachute Division was a Fallschirmjäger (airborne) division of the German military during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.

702nd Infantry Division.summary: The 702nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.
702nd Infantry Division.article: The 702nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.

703rd Infantry Division.summary: The 703rd Infantry Division was a fictitious infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. The troops designated as the 703rd Infantry Division never came close to the strength of an actual division.
703rd Infantry Division.article: The 703rd Infantry Division was a fictitious infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. The troops designated as the 703rd Infantry Division never came close to the strength of an actual division.

707th Infantry Division.summary: The 707th Infantry Division, also known as the 707th Security Division, was a German Army division of World War II. It was formed in May 1941, and destroyed by the Red Army in June 1944.
707th Infantry Division.article: The 707th Infantry Division, also known as the 707th Security Division, was a German Army division of World War II. It was formed in May 1941, and destroyed by the Red Army in June 1944. The unit was mainly used as a rear-security division in German-occupied areas of the Soviet Union. It was responsible for large-scale war crimes, including the deaths of thousands of Jewish civilians.

708th Infantry Division.summary: The 708th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.
708th Infantry Division.article: The 708th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.

708th Volksgrenadier Division.summary: The 708th Volksgrenadier Division, formerly the 708th Infantry Division was a German army division active during World War II. It took part in Operation Northwind, where it was subsequently destroyed.
708th Volksgrenadier Division.article: The 708th Volksgrenadier Division, formerly the 708th Infantry Division was a German army division active during World War II. It took part in Operation Northwind, where it was subsequently destroyed.

709th Infantry Division.summary: The 709th Static Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II. It was raised in May 1941 and used for occupation duties during the German occupation of France in World War II until the Allied invasion.
709th Infantry Division.article: The 709th Static Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II. It was raised in May 1941 and used for occupation duties during the German occupation of France in World War II until the Allied invasion. It was on the Normandy coast when the invasion occurred and so fought in the Battle of Normandy. The division was trapped in the Cotentin Peninsula and destroyed in the defense of Cherbourg.

70th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 70th Infantry Division was a unit of the German Army during World War II. It was formed in 1944 from personnel previously exempted from military service due to stomach disorders or injuries.
70th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 70th Infantry Division was a unit of the German Army during World War II. It was formed in 1944 from personnel previously exempted from military service due to stomach disorders or injuries.

710th Infantry Division.summary: The 710th Infantry Division was a German Army Infantry division in World War II. Formed in May 1941, it spent the majority of the war in occupation duties in Scandinavia before being transferred to the Italian Front in late 1944.
710th Infantry Division.article: The 710th Infantry Division was a German Army Infantry division in World War II. Formed in May 1941, it spent the majority of the war in occupation duties in Scandinavia before being transferred to the Italian Front in late 1944. It participated in fighting against the Soviet Union in Hungary before it withdrew into Austria and surrendered to the Americans in May 1945.

711th Infantry Division.summary: The 711th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.
711th Infantry Division.article: The 711th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.

712th Infantry Division.summary: The 712th Infantry Division was a German Army Infantry division in World War II.
712th Infantry Division.article: The 712th Infantry Division was a German Army Infantry division in World War II.

713th Infantry Division.summary: The 713th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.
713th Infantry Division.article: The 713th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.

715th Infantry Division.summary: The 715th Infantry Division was a German infantry division which fought during World War II.
715th Infantry Division.article: The 715th Infantry Division was a German infantry division which fought during World War II.

716th Static Infantry Division (Wehrmach.summary: The 716th Static Infantry Division was a German World War II infantry division. It was raised on May 2, 1941, and sent to German-occupied France in June 1941.
716th Static Infantry Division (Wehrmach.article: The 716th Static Infantry Division was a German World War II infantry division. It was raised on May 2, 1941, and sent to German-occupied France in June 1941. Many of the division's troops were elderly Germans and conscripts from other German-occupied countries. The division also had some young German conscripts as well. As a bodenständig, it was not equipped with the standard configuration of vehicles and heavy weapons. Much of the division's artillery and anti-tank guns were from captured armaments.

719th Infantry Division.summary: The 719th Infantry Division was a German Army division of World War II.
719th Infantry Division.article: The 719th Infantry Division was a German Army division of World War II.

71st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 71st Infantry Division Kleeblatt was an infantry division of the German Army, raised on 26 August 1939, shortly before the outbreak of World War II, as a division of the 2nd wave of deployment by Infantry Commander 19 in Hildesheim. It fought in Verdun, Stalingrad and Monte Cassino, among others.
71st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 71st Infantry Division Kleeblatt was an infantry division of the German Army, raised on 26 August 1939, shortly before the outbreak of World War II, as a division of the 2nd wave of deployment by Infantry Commander 19 in Hildesheim. It fought in Verdun, Stalingrad and Monte Cassino, among others.

72nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 72nd Infantry Division was formed on 19 September 1939 in Trier from Grenz-Division Trier, a border security unit.
72nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 72nd Infantry Division was formed on 19 September 1939 in Trier from Grenz-Division Trier, a border security unit.

73rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The German 73rd Infantry Division or in German 73. Infanterie-Division was a German military unit which served during World War II.
73rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The German 73rd Infantry Division or in German 73. Infanterie-Division was a German military unit which served during World War II. The division consisted of more than 10,000 soldiers, primarily of the infantry branch, with supporting artillery. The division was only semi-motorized and relied on marching for the infantry units and horse-drawn transport for most of the support equipment, especially the artillery.

74th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 74th Infantry Division was a planned infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. Initially designed to be supplied with recruits by the Milowitz Division as part of the 24th Aufstellungswelle, the creation of the 74th Infantry Division was never realized.
74th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 74th Infantry Division was a planned infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. Initially designed to be supplied with recruits by the Milowitz Division as part of the 24th Aufstellungswelle, the creation of the 74th Infantry Division was never realized. The planned regiments 1031, 1032 and 1033 were not established. The Milowitz Division was established at Milowitz training field on 27 January 1944 and was then sent to the Eastern Front, where its members were assigned to replenish the 320th Infantry Division, 106th Infantry Division and 389th Infantry Division.

75th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 75th Infantry Division was a German infantry division in World War II. It was formed on 26 August 1939.
75th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 75th Infantry Division was a German infantry division in World War II. It was formed on 26 August 1939.

76th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 76th Infantry Division was created on 26 August 1939 together with the 23rd Infantry Division in Potsdam.
76th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 76th Infantry Division was created on 26 August 1939 together with the 23rd Infantry Division in Potsdam.

77th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 77th Infantry Division was a German military unit which served during World War II.
77th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 77th Infantry Division was a German military unit which served during World War II.

78th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 78th Infantry Division, later known as the 78th Assault Division, was a German infantry formation which fought during World War II. After the 78th Assault Division was destroyed near Minsk in July 1944, the 78th Volksgrenadier Division was created.
78th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 78th Infantry Division, later known as the 78th Assault Division, was a German infantry formation which fought during World War II. After the 78th Assault Division was destroyed near Minsk in July 1944, the 78th Volksgrenadier Division was created.

79th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 79th Infantry Division (79. Infanterie-Division) was an infantry division of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht during World War II.
79th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 79th Infantry Division (79. Infanterie-Division) was an infantry division of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht during World War II.

7th Flak Division.summary: The 7th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.
7th Flak Division.article: The 7th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.

7th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 7th Infantry Division was a formation of the German Wehrmacht during World War II.
7th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 7th Infantry Division was a formation of the German Wehrmacht during World War II.

7th Luftwaffe Field Division.summary: The 7th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed using surplus Luftwaffe ground crew in Luftgau III & Luftgau IV at Troop Training Ground Gross-Born, but was soon shifted to Troop Training Ground Mlawa (Mielau) in East Prussia.
7th Luftwaffe Field Division.article: The 7th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed using surplus Luftwaffe ground crew in Luftgau III & Luftgau IV at Troop Training Ground Gross-Born, but was soon shifted to Troop Training Ground Mlawa (Mielau) in East Prussia. Unlike its predecessors the initial rifle complement only included three Independently led Jager Battalions instead of the usual four. It wasn't until December 1942 that the IV Jager Battalion was added. But Like other Luftwaffe Field Divisions initially when first formed the unit's infantry complement contained no regimental headquarters. The Artillery Battalion was composed of two batteries of towed 10.5 cm leFH 18M howitzer batteries with four guns each. While the Third battery was equipped with five Sturmgeschütz III 75mm L24 short barreled Assault Guns. Exactly the same as the 6th Luftwaffe Field Division. The Panzer-Jager Battalion was composed of two companies of 5 cm Pak 38 Anti-Tank Guns, but they were also equipped with the 7.5 cm Pak 40 Anti Tank Guns. The third company were only equipped with the 5 cm Pak 38 Anti Tank Guns. The Flak Battalion contained three Batteries armed with 24 single barreled Flak 38 2 cm Flak 30, Flak 38 and Flakvierling 38, and 4 Flak 36 8.8 cm Flak 18/36/37/41. It served on the Southern Sector of the Eastern Front from late 1942 to May 1943 under Army Group Don, when was disbanded.

7th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 7th Mountain Division was formed through the redesignation of 99th Light Infantry Division, which had fought on the southern sector of the Eastern Front until being withdrawn to Germany in October 1941. In 1942, it was sent to Finland and remained there until the Finnish withdrawal from the war.
7th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 7th Mountain Division was formed through the redesignation of 99th Light Infantry Division, which had fought on the southern sector of the Eastern Front until being withdrawn to Germany in October 1941. In 1942, it was sent to Finland and remained there until the Finnish withdrawal from the war. The Division retreated into Norway where it remained until the end of the War.

7th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 7th Panzer Division was an armored formation of the German Army in World War II. It participated in the Battle of France, the invasion of the Soviet Union, the occupation of Vichy France, and on the Eastern Front until the end of the war.
7th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 7th Panzer Division was an armored formation of the German Army in World War II. It participated in the Battle of France, the invasion of the Soviet Union, the occupation of Vichy France, and on the Eastern Front until the end of the war. The 7th Panzer Division is also known by its nickname, Ghost Division.

7th Parachute Division (Germany).summary: The 7th Parachute Division was a fallschirmjäger (airborne) division of the German military during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.
7th Parachute Division (Germany).article: The 7th Parachute Division was a fallschirmjäger (airborne) division of the German military during the Second World War, active from 1944 to 1945.

81st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 81st Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Army during World War II. It was active from 1939 until 1945 and served primarily on the Eastern Front.
81st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 81st Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Army during World War II. It was active from 1939 until 1945 and served primarily on the Eastern Front.

82nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 82nd Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II. The 82nd was part of the sixth Aufstellungswelle of German infantry divisions.
82nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 82nd Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II. The 82nd was part of the sixth Aufstellungswelle of German infantry divisions.

83rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 83rd Infantry Division was a German reserve and security formation during World War II.
83rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 83rd Infantry Division was a German reserve and security formation during World War II.

84th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 84th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.
84th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 84th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.

85th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 85th Infantry Division was a Wehrmacht division used in the Second World War. It participated in the German defence in the Battle of Normandy, and took part in the German counter-offensive in the Ardennes.
85th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 85th Infantry Division was a Wehrmacht division used in the Second World War. It participated in the German defence in the Battle of Normandy, and took part in the German counter-offensive in the Ardennes.

87th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 87th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1939 to 1945.
87th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 87th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Army during the Second World War, active from 1939 to 1945.

88th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 88th Infantry Division was a major fighting formation of the German Army (Wehrmacht). It was created in December 1939, and first saw combat in the Battle of France, and was then posted to security duties.
88th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 88th Infantry Division was a major fighting formation of the German Army (Wehrmacht). It was created in December 1939, and first saw combat in the Battle of France, and was then posted to security duties. From December 1941, the division was shifted to the southern sector of the Eastern Front, where it fought until February 1944 when it was encircled near Cherkassy and virtually destroyed.

89th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 89th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.
89th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 89th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.

8th Flak Division.summary: The 8th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.
8th Flak Division.article: The 8th Flak Division was a Flak division of the Luftwaffe of Nazi Germany during World War II.

8th Jäger Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The German 8th Infantry Division was formed in Oppeln on 1 October 1934 under the cover name Artillerieführer III which was used until 15 October 1935. It was mobilized in August 1939 and took part in the Invasion of Poland, the Battle of France and Operation Barbarossa, the invasion of the Soviet Union.
8th Jäger Division (Wehrmacht).article: The German 8th Infantry Division was formed in Oppeln on 1 October 1934 under the cover name Artillerieführer III which was used until 15 October 1935. It was mobilized in August 1939 and took part in the Invasion of Poland, the Battle of France and Operation Barbarossa, the invasion of the Soviet Union. On 1 December 1941, it was reorganized and redesignated 8th Light Infantry Division. It was again redesignated on 30 June 1942 as the 8th Jäger Division. It surrendered to the Red Army in Moravia in May 1945.

8th Luftwaffe Field Division.summary: The 8th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed using surplus Luftwaffe ground crew in Luftgau III and Luftgau IV at Troop Training Ground Mlawa (Mielau) in East Prussia.
8th Luftwaffe Field Division.article: The 8th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed using surplus Luftwaffe ground crew in Luftgau III and Luftgau IV at Troop Training Ground Mlawa (Mielau) in East Prussia. From Flieger-Regiment 42, which was an expansion of Flieger Ersatz-Abteilung 42. This unit had been created in April 1939 as an air force replacement battalion. In 1941 was stationed at Salzwedel in Luftgau XI in Hannover. In October 1942 it was disbanded and its two battalions became the core of the 8th Luftwaffe Field Division. Like its predecessors the initial rifle complement included Four Independently led Jager Battalions no regimental headquarters. Two regimental headquarters, Jager-Regiments 15 and 16 were supposed to have been formed but because the formation was committed so rapidly and destroyed so quickly, this never occurred. The Artillery Battalion was composed of two batteries of towed Czech made Škoda 75 mm Model 15 mountain guns with four guns each. While the Third battery was equipped with five Sturmgeschutz III 75mm L24 short barreled Assault Guns. The rest of the Division consisted of a Panzer-Jager Battalion, a signals company, an engineer company, and a supply column. Plus the Flak Battalion contained three Batteries armed with 24 single barreled Flak 38 2 cm Flak 30, Flak 38 and Flakvierling 38, and 4 Flak 36 8.8 cm Flak 18/36/37/41 guns. It served on the Southern Sector of the Eastern Front along the Chir and Don River sectors from late 1942 to April/May 1943 as part of Armee-Abteilung Hollidt. when it was disbanded and the remnant parts were absorbed by 15th Luftwaffe Field Division.

8th Mountain Division.summary: The 8th Mountain Division was formed on 27 February 1945 by the redesignation of the 157th Mountain Division, which itself had been formed from the 157th Infantry Division in September 1944, and which had participated in operations against the maquis of Vercors. The division was stationed in France until the Italian surrender when it then moved to Italy taking 5,772 prisoners in two days during the disarmament of the Italian Army.
8th Mountain Division.article: The 8th Mountain Division was formed on 27 February 1945 by the redesignation of the 157th Mountain Division, which itself had been formed from the 157th Infantry Division in September 1944, and which had participated in operations against the maquis of Vercors. The division was stationed in France until the Italian surrender when it then moved to Italy taking 5,772 prisoners in two days during the disarmament of the Italian Army. The division remained in Italy for the rest of the war and surrendered to the Americans in April, 1945.

8th Panzer Division.summary: The 8th Panzer Division was a formation of the Wehrmacht Heer. The division was formed by reorganising the 3rd Light Division in October 1939.
8th Panzer Division.article: The 8th Panzer Division was a formation of the Wehrmacht Heer. The division was formed by reorganising the 3rd Light Division in October 1939. It was transferred to the west and fought in the Battle of France, in May 1940, and the German invasion of the Balkans in April 1941. Soon after the division advanced towards Leningrad under Army Group North in Operation Barbarossa, and would remain on the eastern front for the remainder of the war. Staying on defensive fronts, it saw action in the relief of Kholm in 1942, Orel and the withdrawals of Army Group Centre in 1943, until transferred to Army group South. The division then fought in a series of retrograde movements, back through Ukraine, into Hungary and finally into Silesia and surrender in May 1945.

8th Parachute Division (Germany).summary: The 8th Parachute Division was a division of the German military during the Second World War, active in 1945. It was fallschirmjäger in title only and was trained and served as a regular infantry unit.
8th Parachute Division (Germany).article: The 8th Parachute Division was a division of the German military during the Second World War, active in 1945. It was fallschirmjäger in title only and was trained and served as a regular infantry unit.

90th Light Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 90th Light Infantry Division was a light infantry division of the German Army during World War II that served in North Africa as well as Sardinia and Italy. The division played a major role in most of the actions against the British Eighth Army in the Western Desert Campaign and eventually surrendered to the Allies in the final stages of the Tunisia Campaign in May 1943.
90th Light Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 90th Light Infantry Division was a light infantry division of the German Army during World War II that served in North Africa as well as Sardinia and Italy. The division played a major role in most of the actions against the British Eighth Army in the Western Desert Campaign and eventually surrendered to the Allies in the final stages of the Tunisia Campaign in May 1943. It was re-constituted later in 1943 and deployed to Sardinia and when the expected Allied invasion of Sardinia failed to materialise, the division was moved to Italy. It was engaged in actions against the Allies in Italy from 1943 to April 1945 when the division was listed as "destroyed" in the Po River valley.

91st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 91st Air Landing Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.
91st Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 91st Air Landing Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.

92nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 92nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.
92nd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 92nd Infantry Division was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II.

93rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 93rd Infantry Division was a German infantry division that was formed in the fall of 1939. The division fought in the Second World War in both the Battle of France and on the Eastern Front.
93rd Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 93rd Infantry Division was a German infantry division that was formed in the fall of 1939. The division fought in the Second World War in both the Battle of France and on the Eastern Front. It was ultimately destroyed by the Red Army in March 1945 while defending East Prussia.

94th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 94th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.
94th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 94th Infantry Division was a German Army infantry division in World War II.

95th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 95th Infantry Division was a German division in World War II. It was formed on 19 September 1939 in Wildflecken and Hammelburg.
95th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 95th Infantry Division was a German division in World War II. It was formed on 19 September 1939 in Wildflecken and Hammelburg.

96th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 96th Infantry Division was a German division deployed during World War II. It was formed on 25 September 1939 in Bergen as part of the 5th wave (aufstellungswelle).
96th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 96th Infantry Division was a German division deployed during World War II. It was formed on 25 September 1939 in Bergen as part of the 5th wave (aufstellungswelle).

97th Jäger Division.summary: The 97th Jäger Division was a light infantry Division of the German Army during World War II. It can trace its origins to the 97th (Light) Infantry Division which was formed in December 1940.
97th Jäger Division.article: The 97th Jäger Division was a light infantry Division of the German Army during World War II. It can trace its origins to the 97th (Light) Infantry Division which was formed in December 1940. It was then redesignated the 97th Jäger Division in July 1942.

98th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 98th Infantry Division was created on 18 September 1939 in Grafenwöhr. It was destroyed on the Crimea in May 1944 and reformed on 5 June 1944.
98th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 98th Infantry Division was created on 18 September 1939 in Grafenwöhr. It was destroyed on the Crimea in May 1944 and reformed on 5 June 1944.

99th Light Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 99th Light Infantry Division was raised in November 1940 and remained in training at Bad Kissingen until the summer of 1941. In June it took part in Operation Barbarossa—the German invasion of the Soviet Union—and operated in Poland and Ukraine under Army Group South until the fall, when it was withdrawn to Germany for reorganization as the 7th Mountain Division.
99th Light Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 99th Light Infantry Division was raised in November 1940 and remained in training at Bad Kissingen until the summer of 1941. In June it took part in Operation Barbarossa—the German invasion of the Soviet Union—and operated in Poland and Ukraine under Army Group South until the fall, when it was withdrawn to Germany for reorganization as the 7th Mountain Division.

9th Flak Division.summary: The 9th Flak Division was a Flak division of the German Luftwaffe in World War II which saw action on the Eastern Front. It is most notable for its role in the Battle of Stalingrad.
9th Flak Division.article: The 9th Flak Division was a Flak division of the German Luftwaffe in World War II which saw action on the Eastern Front. It is most notable for its role in the Battle of Stalingrad. Another formation with the same name was deployed to the Eastern Front and saw combat in the Kuban bridgehead.

9th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 9th Infantry Division was a formation of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht.
9th Infantry Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 9th Infantry Division was a formation of Nazi Germany's Wehrmacht.

9th Luftwaffe Field Division.summary: The 9th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed in October 1942 in Eastern Prussia, in the Arys Troop Maneuver Area, under the command of Oberst Hans Erdmann.
9th Luftwaffe Field Division.article: The 9th Luftwaffe Field Division was an infantry division of the Luftwaffe branch of the Wehrmacht that fought in World War II. It was formed in October 1942 in Eastern Prussia, in the Arys Troop Maneuver Area, under the command of Oberst Hans Erdmann. Intended to serve as infantry, its personnel were largely drawn from the Luftwaffes 62nd Air Regiment. According to Official records this Division was the first to be formed employing two Regimental Headquarters. It had Six Jager Battalions instead of the usual Four. This meant that both regiments had a complement of three infantry battalions apiece. The Artillery Regiment was supposed to contain three artillery battalions but strangely there was only two. The I Artillery Battalion was composed of three Batteries of 122 mm howitzer M1938 (M-30) Towed. The II Artillery Battalion was composed of three batteries of 76 mm divisional gun M1936 (F-22) guns towed. The Panzer-Jager Battalion contained two companies of Towed 7.5 cm Pak 40 Anti Tank Guns. A Fusilier Battalion of four companies, which acted as the Divisions Reconnaissance. And a Pioneer battalion which contained only one company the third.

9th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 9th Mountain Division was the name given to two separate German military divisions by accident in 1945.
9th Mountain Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 9th Mountain Division was the name given to two separate German military divisions by accident in 1945.

9th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht).summary: The 9th Panzer Division was a panzer division of the German Army during World War II. It came into existence after 4th Light Division was reorganized in January 1940.
9th Panzer Division (Wehrmacht).article: The 9th Panzer Division was a panzer division of the German Army during World War II. It came into existence after 4th Light Division was reorganized in January 1940. The division was headquartered in Vienna, in the German military district Wehrkreis XVII.

9th Parachute Division (Germany).summary: The 9th Parachute Division was one of the final parachute divisions to be raised by Nazi Germany during World War II. The division was destroyed during the Battle of Berlin in April 1945.
9th Parachute Division (Germany).article: The 9th Parachute Division was one of the final parachute divisions to be raised by Nazi Germany during World War II. The division was destroyed during the Battle of Berlin in April 1945.

Division von Broich/von Manteuffel.summary: Division von Broich was a German provisional infantry division active in 1942–43 in North Africa. It was created in November 1942 and named after its notable commander Friedrich von Broich.
Division von Broich/von Manteuffel.article:
Division von Broich was a German provisional infantry division active in 1942–43 in North Africa. It was created in November 1942 and named after its notable commander Friedrich von Broich. In February 1943, the division was renamed Division von Manteuffel after its new commander, Generalmajor Hasso von Manteuffel.
Despite its makeshift nature, the unit proved itself as a capable force, taking part in numerous actions until forced to capitulate with the rest of Army Group Africa in May 1943.

1st Fallschirm-Panzer Division Hermann G.summary: The Fallschirm-Panzer-Division 1. Hermann Göring was a German elite Luftwaffe armoured division.
1st Fallschirm-Panzer Division Hermann G.article: The Fallschirm-Panzer-Division 1. Hermann Göring was a German elite Luftwaffe armoured division. The HG saw action in France, North Africa, Sicily, Italy and on the Eastern Front during World War II. The division began as a battalion-sized police unit in 1933. Over time it grew into a regiment, brigade, division, and finally was combined with the Parachute-Panzergrenadier Division 2 Hermann Göring on 1 May 1944 to form a Panzer corps under the name Reichsmarschall. It surrendered to the Red Army near Dresden on 8 May 1945.

Fallschirm-Panzergrenadier Division 2 He.summary: Fallschirm-Panzergrenadier-Division 2 "Hermann Göring" was a German military unit formed on 24 September 1944 in the area of Radom. It subsequently was joined with the Fallschirm-Panzer Division 1 Hermann Göring to form the Fallschirm Panzer Corps Hermann Göring.
Fallschirm-Panzergrenadier Division 2 He.article: Fallschirm-Panzergrenadier-Division 2 "Hermann Göring" was a German military unit formed on 24 September 1944 in the area of Radom. It subsequently was joined with the Fallschirm-Panzer Division 1 Hermann Göring to form the Fallschirm Panzer Corps Hermann Göring. After heavy fighting against the Red Army in Prussia, Pomerania, Silesia, and Saxony, the Corps surrendered to Soviet troops on May 8, 1945.

Fortress Division Frankfurt/Oder.summary: The Frankfurt/Oder Fortress Division was a "fortress" division of the German Army (Heer) during the Second World War, active during the last months of the war in 1945.
Fortress Division Frankfurt/Oder.article: The Frankfurt/Oder Fortress Division was a "fortress" division of the German Army (Heer) during the Second World War, active during the last months of the war in 1945.

Fortress Division Swinemünde.summary: Fortress Division Swinemünde was an ad hoc formation under the command of Baron Hermmann Von Pfel, and organized by Seekommandant Pommern in the port of Swinemünde. The division was created in 1945 primarily from Kriegsmarine personnel, although some Luftwaffe personnel were used as well.
Fortress Division Swinemünde.article: Fortress Division Swinemünde was an ad hoc formation under the command of Baron Hermmann Von Pfel, and organized by Seekommandant Pommern in the port of Swinemünde. The division was created in 1945 primarily from Kriegsmarine personnel, although some Luftwaffe personnel were used as well. The Army component of the Swinemünde fortress division was destroyed by the Soviet 5th Guards Army in Lower Silesia near Breslau.

Fortress Division Warsaw.summary: The Fortress Division Warsaw was a fortress division of the Army Group A of the German Wehrmacht in World War II.
Fortress Division Warsaw.article: The Fortress Division Warsaw was a fortress division of the Army Group A of the German Wehrmacht in World War II.

Freiwilligen-Stamm-Division.summary: The Freiwilligen-Stamm-Division was a Wehrmacht infantry division during World War II. It was created on 1 February 1944 in Southern France.
Freiwilligen-Stamm-Division.article: The Freiwilligen-Stamm-Division was a Wehrmacht infantry division during World War II. It was created on 1 February 1944 in Southern France. The Division was a so-called Ostlegion, which means its personnel was made up from volunteers from the Soviet Union. Specifically Freiwilligen-Stamm-Division consisted of Turkic, Azerbaijani, Georgian, Tartar, Cossack, Armenian, Iranian and other Soviet volunteers, spread over five regiments. The primary purpose of the division were anti-partisan operations against the French Resistance.

Infantry Division Ferdinand von Schill.summary: The Infantry Division Ferdinand von Schill was an infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was formed in April 1945, within a month of the end of the war, and was among the last divisions fielded by the German army during the war.
Infantry Division Ferdinand von Schill.article: The Infantry Division Ferdinand von Schill was an infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was formed in April 1945, within a month of the end of the war, and was among the last divisions fielded by the German army during the war.

Infantry Division Friedrich Ludwig Jahn.summary: The Infantry Division Friedrich Ludwig Jahn was an infantry division of the German army during World War II. It was formed in the final weeks of the war, and existed between late March and early May 1945.
Infantry Division Friedrich Ludwig Jahn.article: The Infantry Division Friedrich Ludwig Jahn was an infantry division of the German army during World War II. It was formed in the final weeks of the war, and existed between late March and early May 1945.

Infantry Division Güstrow.summary: Infantry Division Güstrow was an infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. Its assembly in late April 1945 was undertaken too late to impact the war, and the division never saw combat.
Infantry Division Güstrow.article: Infantry Division Güstrow was an infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. Its assembly in late April 1945 was undertaken too late to impact the war, and the division never saw combat.

Infantry Division Hamburg.summary: The Infantry Division Hamburg was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was assigned the ordinal number 324, but was no longer known as the 324th Infantry Division after 10 March 1945.
Infantry Division Hamburg.article: The Infantry Division Hamburg was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was assigned the ordinal number 324, but was no longer known as the 324th Infantry Division after 10 March 1945. The division existed only in early March 1945.

Infantry Division Jütland.summary: The 325th Infantry Division, generally known as Infantry Division "Jütland", was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was formed in March 1945, as one of the Wehrmacht's final emergency formations of the war.
Infantry Division Jütland.article: The 325th Infantry Division, generally known as Infantry Division "Jütland", was an infantry division of the German Heer during World War II. It was formed in March 1945, as one of the Wehrmacht's final emergency formations of the war. The division's deployment was never finished.

Infantry Division Kurland.summary: The Infantry Division Courland was an infantry division of the German army during World War II.
Infantry Division Kurland.article: The Infantry Division Courland was an infantry division of the German army during World War II.

Infantry Division Schlageter.summary: The Infantry Division Schlageter was an infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was formed in late March 1945 as one of the last new divisions of the Wehrmacht before the end of the war.
Infantry Division Schlageter.article: The Infantry Division Schlageter was an infantry division of the German Wehrmacht during World War II. It was formed in late March 1945 as one of the last new divisions of the Wehrmacht before the end of the war.

Infantry Division Theodor Körner.summary: The "Theodor Körner" Infantry Division was a German army infantry division during World War II. The unit consisted mainly of members of the Reich Labour Service (RAD) and existed in April and May 1945.
Infantry Division Theodor Körner.article: The "Theodor Körner" Infantry Division was a German army infantry division during World War II. The unit consisted mainly of members of the Reich Labour Service (RAD) and existed in April and May 1945.

Panzer Division Clausewitz.summary: Panzer-Division Clausewitz was a German panzer division during World War II, named for Carl von Clausewitz.
Panzer Division Clausewitz.article: Panzer-Division Clausewitz was a German panzer division during World War II, named for Carl von Clausewitz.

Panzer Division Feldherrnhalle 2.summary: Panzer Division Feldherrnhalle 2 was a late World War II German Wehrmacht panzer division. Commanded by Franz Bäke, it was formed in March 1945 of the remnants of other divisions and stationed with a home base at Wehrkreis XX.
Panzer Division Feldherrnhalle 2.article: Panzer Division Feldherrnhalle 2 was a late World War II German Wehrmacht panzer division. Commanded by Franz Bäke, it was formed in March 1945 of the remnants of other divisions and stationed with a home base at Wehrkreis XX. It was moved to Slovakia in 1945, where it fought its only battles. It was later disbanded.

Panzer Division Holstein.summary: Panzer-Division Holstein was an armoured division of the German army during World War 2. It was in formed on 10 February 1945 through the acquisition of the mobile parts of the 233 Reserve Panzer Division.
Panzer Division Holstein.article: Panzer-Division Holstein was an armoured division of the German army during World War 2. It was in formed on 10 February 1945 through the acquisition of the mobile parts of the 233 Reserve Panzer Division. It suffered heavy losses in the area southwest of Kolberg, and the remnants were absorbed by the 18th Panzergrenadier Division on 26 March 1945.

Panzer Division Jüterbog.summary: The Panzer Division Jüterbog was an armoured division of the Wehrmacht during World War II. Created on 20 February 1945, it was active for a short period of time.
Panzer Division Jüterbog.article: The Panzer Division Jüterbog was an armoured division of the Wehrmacht during World War II. Created on 20 February 1945, it was active for a short period of time.

Panzer Division Kempf.summary: The Panzer Division "Kempf", also East Prussia Panzer Formation was an ad hoc combined arms formation consisting of regular German Army personnel and SS. It was created for operations out of East Prussia during the Invasion of Poland in 1939.
Panzer Division Kempf.article: The Panzer Division "Kempf", also East Prussia Panzer Formation was an ad hoc combined arms formation consisting of regular German Army personnel and SS. It was created for operations out of East Prussia during the Invasion of Poland in 1939. The formation was generally called Panzer Division "Kempf" after General der Panzertruppe Werner Kempf assumed command, though it was only about half the strength of the other Panzer Divisions of the time.

Panzer Division Müncheberg.summary: Panzer-Division Müncheberg was a German panzer division which saw action on the Eastern Front around Berlin during World War II.
Panzer Division Müncheberg.article: Panzer-Division Müncheberg was a German panzer division which saw action on the Eastern Front around Berlin during World War II.

Panzer Division Tatra.summary: Panzer Division Tatra was an armored division of the German Army formed in Moravia in August 1944.
